title_en: Marriage and Governance in Al-Hawi al-Kabir
title_id: Pernikahan dan Pemerintahan dalam Al-Hawi al-Kabir
content_id:
Pembahasan Al-Hawi al-Kabir tentang hukum pernikahan dan, secara unik mengingat keahlian al-Mawardi, konteks yang lebih luas dari pemerintahan Islam — peran penguasa dalam urusan keluarga, pengawasan yudisial atas akad nikah, dan tanggung jawab negara dalam menegakkan hukum Islam — memberikan bab ini dimensi yang tidak ditemukan dalam kebanyakan manual fikih.

Pembahasan al-Mawardi tentang akad nikah mengikuti posisi mazhab Syafi'i tentang syarat wali, syarat dua saksi, mahar, dan mahram dengan ketelitian khasnya. Ia mengkaji syarat wali secara mendalam khusus, terlibat dengan posisi mazhab Hanafi bahwa wanita dewasa dapat mengontrak pernikahannya sendiri dan menjelaskan mengapa mazhab Syafi'i mempertahankan peran wali sebagai syarat keabsahan. Argumennya mengacu pada hadis kenabian 'Tidak ada nikah tanpa wali' (Ahmad, Ibn Majah) dan pada tujuan sosial dari institusi wali: melindungi kepentingan jangka panjang wanita dari keputusan-keputusan yang dibuat di bawah tekanan emosional atau sosial.

Tentang mahram, Al-Hawi al-Kabir menyajikan pembahasan paling luas tentang larangan-larangan Qur'ani (an-Nisa 4:22–23) dalam tradisi Syafi'i. Al-Mawardi mengkaji dasar setiap larangan, perluasan setiap larangan ke kasus-kasus yang analogis melalui qiyas, dan pertanyaan-pertanyaan kompleks yang muncul di batas larangan-larangan tersebut — seperti apakah pernikahan dengan wanita dari Ahli Kitab dilarang bagi pria Muslim selama masa konflik dengan bangsa non-Muslim, dan apa putusan mengenai pernikahan dengan orang murtad atau mereka yang status keagamaannya dipertanyakan.

Kontribusi unik al-Mawardi dalam Al-Hawi al-Kabir, dengan mengacu pada keahliannya sebagai ahli fikih sekaligus ahli teori politik (pengarang Al-Ahkam as-Sultaniyyah), adalah perhatian yang ia berikan kepada peran negara Islam dalam menegakkan hukum keluarga. Penguasa dan hakim bertanggung jawab untuk memastikan bahwa akad-akad nikah memenuhi syarat-syaratnya, kewajiban nafkah ditegakkan, perceraian dilaksanakan sesuai prosedur Islam, dan hak-hak perempuan dan anak-anak dalam institusi keluarga dilindungi. Dimensi pemerintahan ini — negara sebagai penjamin hukum keluarga Islam — lebih eksplisit dikembangkan dalam Al-Hawi al-Kabir daripada dalam kebanyakan manual fikih yang murni bersifat yurisprudensial.

Bab talak dalam Al-Hawi al-Kabir mencakup posisi-posisi Syafi'i dengan analisis komparatif yang luas. Al-Mawardi mengkaji pertanyaan talak tiga sekaligus — apakah dihitung sebagai tiga talak yang tidak dapat dirujuk (posisi Syafi'i dan mayoritas Sunni) atau sebagai satu talak yang dapat dirujuk (posisi minoritas). Keterlibatannya dengan bukti hadis di kedua sisi bersifat menyeluruh, dan ia mempertahankan posisi mayoritas sambil mengakui bobot nyata dari pandangan minoritas.

Pembahasan al-Mawardi tentang iddah (masa tunggu) mencakup diskusi penting tentang tujuan-tujuannya: menetapkan apakah wanita sedang hamil (untuk menentukan nasab anak yang ada), memberikan waktu untuk rekonsiliasi dalam kasus talak raj'i, dan menghormati pernikahan sebelumnya melalui periode transisi yang bermartabat. Memahami tujuan-tujuan ini membantu menjelaskan mengapa iddah memiliki durasi spesifik seperti yang ada dan mengapa hal itu tidak dapat dinegosiasikan — ia melayani tujuan-tujuan yang melampaui kenyamanan salah satu pihak. Pendekatan purposif terhadap analisis hukum ini, yang merupakan ciri khas dari metode yurisprudensial al-Mawardi yang canggih, adalah salah satu fitur yang menjadikan Al-Hawi al-Kabir sebuah tonggak dalam pemikiran hukum Islam.
content_en:
Al-Hawi al-Kabir's treatment of marriage law and, uniquely given al-Mawardi's expertise, its broader context of Islamic governance — the role of the ruler in family affairs, judicial oversight of marriage contracts, and the state's responsibilities in upholding Islamic law — give this chapter a dimension not found in most fiqh manuals.

Al-Mawardi's treatment of the marriage contract follows the Shafi'i school's positions on the wali requirement, the two-witness requirement, the mahr, and the prohibited degrees with characteristic thoroughness. He examines the wali requirement in particular depth, engaging with the Hanafi school's position that an adult woman may contract her own marriage and explaining why the Shafi'i school maintains the wali's role as a condition of validity. His argument draws on the prophetic hadith 'No marriage without a wali' (Ahmad, Ibn Majah) and on the social purpose of the wali institution: protecting the woman's long-term interests against decisions made under emotional or social pressure.

On the prohibited degrees, Al-Hawi al-Kabir provides perhaps the most extensive classical treatment of the Quranic prohibitions (al-Nisa 4:22–23) in the Shafi'i tradition. Al-Mawardi examines the basis for each prohibition, the extension of each prohibition to analogous cases through qiyas, and the complex questions that arise at the boundary of the prohibitions — such as whether marriage to a woman from the People of the Book is prohibited to a Muslim man during times of conflict with non-Muslim nations, and what the ruling is regarding marriage to apostates or those of questionable religious status.

Al-Mawardi's unique contribution in Al-Hawi al-Kabir, drawing on his expertise as both a jurist and a political theorist (author of Al-Ahkam as-Sultaniyyah), is the attention he gives to the role of the Islamic state in upholding family law. The ruler and judges are responsible for ensuring that marriage contracts meet their conditions, that maintenance obligations are enforced, that divorce is carried out according to Islamic procedure, and that the rights of women and children within the family institution are protected. This governance dimension — the state as guarantor of Islamic family law — is more explicitly developed in Al-Hawi al-Kabir than in most purely jurisprudential fiqh manuals.

The chapter on talaq in Al-Hawi al-Kabir covers the Shafi'i positions with extensive comparative analysis. Al-Mawardi examines the question of the triple talaq in one session — whether it counts as three irrevocable divorces (the Shafi'i and majority Sunni position) or as one revocable divorce (a minority position). His engagement with the hadith evidence on both sides is thorough, and he defends the majority position while acknowledging the genuine weight of the minority view.

Al-Mawardi's treatment of the iddah (waiting period) includes an important discussion of its purposes: establishing whether the woman is pregnant (to determine paternity of any child), allowing time for reconciliation in cases of revocable divorce, and honoring the prior marriage through a dignified period of transition. Understanding these purposes helps explain why the iddah has the specific duration it does and why it is non-negotiable — it serves objectives that go beyond the convenience of either party. This purposive approach to legal analysis, characteristic of al-Mawardi's sophisticated jurisprudential method, is one of the features that makes Al-Hawi al-Kabir a landmark in Islamic legal thought.
content_ar: Al-Hawi al-Kabir's treatment of marriage law and, uniquely given al-Mawardi's expertise, its broader context of Islamic governance — the role of the ruler in الأسرة affairs, judicial oversight of marriage contracts, and the state's responsibilities in upholding Islamic law — give this الباب a dimension not found in most الفقه manuals. Al-Mawardi's treatment of the marriage contract follows the الشافعي school's positions on the wali requirement, the two-witness requirement, the mahr, and the prohibited degrees with characteristic thoroughness. He examines the wali requirement وبالأخص depth, engaging with the الحنفي school's position that an adult woman may contract her own marriage and explaining why the الشافعي school maintains the wali's role as a condition of validity. His argument draws on the prophetic الحديث 'No marriage without a wali' (أحمد, ابن ماجه) and on the social purpose of the wali institution: protecting the woman's long-term interests against decisions made under emotional or social pressure. On the prohibited degrees, Al-Hawi al-Kabir provides perhaps the most extensive الكلاسيكي treatment of the القرآني prohibitions (al-Nisa 4:22–23) in the الشافعي التراث. Al-Mawardi examines the basis for each prohibition, the extension of each prohibition to analogous cases through القياس, and the complex questions that arise at the boundary of the prohibitions — مثل whether marriage to a woman from the People of the الكتاب is prohibited to a مسلم man خلال times of conflict with non-مسلم nations, and what the ruling is فيما يخص marriage to apostates or those of questionable religious status. Al-Mawardi's unique contribution in Al-Hawi al-Kabir, drawing on his expertise as both a jurist and a political theorist (author of Al-Ahkam as-Sultaniyyah), is the attention he gives to the role of the Islamic state in upholding الأسرة law. The ruler and judges are responsible for ensuring that marriage contracts meet their conditions, that maintenance obligations are enforced, that divorce is carried out وفقاً لـ Islamic procedure, and that the الحقوق of women and الأولاد within the الأسرة institution are protected. This governance dimension — the state as guarantor of Islamic الأسرة law — is more explicitly developed in Al-Hawi al-Kabir than in most purely jurisprudential الفقه manuals. The الباب on talaq in Al-Hawi al-Kabir covers the الشافعي positions with extensive comparative analysis. Al-Mawardi examines the question of the triple talaq in one session — whether it counts as three irrevocable divorces (the الشافعي and majority Sunni position) or as one revocable divorce (a minority position). His engagement with the الحديث evidence on both sides is thorough, and he defends the majority position بينما acknowledging the genuine weight of the minority view.
